Alright, you're back and it's time to move on to number eight, the last in this series here, which is that your script is actually part of a series of scripts. It's not just one long script. In other words, we're going to have our introductory script, which could be for qualifying for prospecting, right? That's how we start off. And that includes the first four seconds, a brief explanation about your company. I'll get to this in the next section here, the specifics here, but you have that opening script, right? You ask your questions, right? And then from there, depending on how many different calls are in your sales cycle, you might then just go into a prospecting situation here, right? And then when it's done, you'll end the call, send someone some information and set up the next encounter. That's in a two-call system, right? Now, if you're in a one-call system, what you would do here is you essentially would go through the whole opening introduction. You ask your prospecting questions, right? Asking for permission first, of course, right? Just the right tonality, use all the rules you follow, right? And then at the end, you then transition into the main body of your script, your presentation, right? And then you ask for the order for the first time, right? And you would end there. But remember, even in that one-call situation, right? You're going to have, look at it as a series of scripts. You have your qualifying, then you have your main body, your presentation, then you're going to have your looping scripts. So you want to have at least three ways to talk about yourself. You want to have three ways to talk about your company and three ways to explain why the product is awesome both logically and emotionally. And when I say the word ways, what I'm referring to here is what we get to next is called language patterns. You've heard me say this many times already, right? But now I'm going to really dig into what language patterns really are and how you construct these language patterns to make a front to back awesome straight line presentation. But again, just remember here that there is this subject of the sale cycle. I'm going to deal with that separately, a separate module here, right? But the point is based on what your sale cycle is, your scripts will adjust to that. But either way, whether it's on one call, two calls, three calls or four calls, all right? And I'm really against more than three calls here unless it's a very complex sale with many multiple decision makers. Either way, I want you to remember that your scripts are always a series of scripts. And if you put them from front to back, they make one perfect cohesive straight line presentation. All right? And that's the eighth and final point. And with that now, I'm going to give you a short quiz. It was a short lesson. I'm going to separate that from energy and benefits out because that's a lot, right? But yeah, let's take a short quiz here. And when you come back, we're going to start digging into these exact language patterns. I'm going to show you how you go about constructing a script. All right? I'll see you soon.
